text: BAILDEN, or BAILDON, a township in the p. of Orley, W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. N of Bradford. Area 2,940 acres. Pop. 3,280.
BAILENGRIOS. See BEILIGRIES.
BAILEY, a township in the p. of Mitton, Lancashire. Pop. 298.
BAILEYSVILLE, a town of Maine, U. S., in Washington co., on the St. Croix river. Pop. 329.
BAILIE, a township in the p. of Bewcastle, Cumberland, 5 m. NE of Longtown. Pop. 431.
BAILIEBOROUGH, a parish in co. Cavan. Area 12,416 acres. Pop. 6,984.—Its principal village, of the same name, is 53 m. WNW of Kingscourt.

BAILIQUE, or Penitencia, an island of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, in the embouchure of the Amazon, near the confluence of the Araguari. It is about 6 m. in circumf. and is inhabited by about 100 Indian families.
BAILLEE, a town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, and cant. of Grez-en-Bouere, 16 m. NE of Chateau-Gontier. Pop. 763.

BAILLEUL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, and arrond. of Hazebrouck. The cant. comprises 9 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 27,728.—The town, which is open, is situated on a rising ground, 9 m. E of Hazebrouck, and 16 m. WNW of Lille. It is tolerably well-built, and possesses manufactories of lace, thread, tape, linen, ticking, rateens, china, pottery, sugar from beet-root, oil, tobacco, and black-soap, tile-works, tanneries, breweries, and an extensive distillery of brandy. A species of cheese much esteemed is peculiar to the canton. There are 5 annual fairs, at which grain forms the principal article of traffic. Pop. 9,823.

BAILLEUL (LE), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, and cant. of Malicorne, 5 m. NNW of La Fleche. It was formerly a flourishing commercial town. Pop. 1,067.—Also a town of France, in the dep. of Orne, 6 m. N of Argentine, celebrated as the birth-place of Baliol, competitor with Robert Bruce for the crown of Scotland.
BAILLY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nicore, in the cant. and I m. S of Donzy. It has manufactories of plate-iron.
BAILLY CAPE, a headland on the E coast of Van Diemen's Land, in S lat. 42° 16'.
BAILY ISLANDS, the southern cluster of the Bonin archipelago.
BAIMAK, a town of Hungary, co. of Bacs, 13 m. SW of Theresianopel, and 40 SW of Szegedin.
BAIMBERG, a town of Hesse-Darmstadt, near the l. bank of the Apfel, 9 m. NW of Alzey, and 19 m. SSW of Maynz.

BAIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, and arrond. of Redon. The cant. comprises 6 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 13,812. The town is 21 m. S of Rennes, and has manufactories of hair-cloth and serge, and tanneries. Pop. 3,490.
BAINA, or Bajna, a town of Hungary, in the co. and 12 m. SW of Gran, and 25 m. ESE of Komorn. It is now nearly deserted.

BAINBRIDGE, a township in the p. of Aysgarth, in the N. R. of Yorkshire. Area 14,210 acres. Pop. 786.—Also a township of New York, U. S., in Chenango co., 104 m. SW of Albany, watered by the Susquehanna and its tributaries. Its soil is fertile, and its pasturage extremely rich. Pop. 3,324.—Also a village of Pennsylvania, U.S., in Lancaster co., 20 m. SE of Harrisburg, consisting of about 20 houses, on the W bank of the Susquehanna.—Also a village of Ohio, U. S., in Ross co., 69 m. S of Columbus, near the falls of Paint creek. It consists of about 60 houses, and contains two churches.— Also a township of Ohio, U. S., in Geauga co., watered by the Chagrin and its tributaries. Pop. 988.—Also a township in Michigan, U. S., in Berrian co. Pop. 251.—Also a village in Georgia, U. S., capital of Decatur co., 188 m. SSW of Milledgeville, on the E bank of the Flint. It has a jail, court-house, &c.
BAINCTHUN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, and cant. of Boulogne. Pop. 1,687.
BAINDER, or BEINDER, a town of Asia Minor, in the pash. of Anatolia, and sanj. of Aidin, 17 m. ESE of Smyrna. The environs afford a considerable quantity of cotton.

BAINDT, or Baienbach, a town of Wurtemburg, in the circle of Bodensee, 5 m. NNW of Ravensburg. It contains a castle of the princes of Salm-Reifferscheid-Dyk, previously an imperial Cistercian abbey, founded in 1238.

BAINS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, and cant. of Redon. Pop. 3,915 .- Also a com. of France, in the dep. of Haute- Loire, and cant. of Solignac. Pop. 1,155.—Also a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Vosges, and arrond. of Epinal. The cant. comprises 13 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 11,174.—The town is 16 m. SSW of Epinal, and is celebrated for its thermal springs and baths. It has some nail-manufactories, and extensive iron and wire-works.

BAINS (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, and cant. of Arles, on the r. bank of the Lech, 3 m. WSW of Ceret. There is a well-garrisoned fort here. Several thermal springs and baths exist here. Of the former two are enclosed in a basin supposed to be of Roman construction. BAI

BAINS-DU-MONT-DORE (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, and cant. of Besse, on the r. bank of the Dordogne, 19 m. SW of Clermont. On the opposite side of the river—which is here crossed by an iron bridge—is a remarkable porphyritic rock, called the Capuchin. Thermal springs are numerous here; and the modern bathestablishment—which occupies the site of an ancient Roman structure—is a handsome edifice. The com. also contains extensive alum-works. Pop. 1,010.

BAINS-DE-RENNES (LES), or DE-MONTFERRAND, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, and cant. of Couiza, on the Sals, 11 m. E of Limoux. There are five mineral springs here, three of which are thermal, and the baths are much frequented. Numerous Roman remains exist in the locality. Pop. 493.

BAINTON, a township in the p. of Ufford, Northamptonshire, 5 m. E by N of Wansford. Area 960 acres. Pop. 161.—Also a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. SW of Great Duffield. Area, with the township of Neswick, 3,280 acres. Pop. 452.

BAIRAMITSH, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak and 60 m. SW of Biga. The houses are generally well and regularly built. Numerous Roman antiquities have been discovered in the environs, and their materials used in the construction of the town.

BAIRAM-KALESI, or BEIRAM, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak and on the N side of the gulf of Adramyti, 15 m. E of Cape Baba. It occupies the site of the ancient Assus, of which many fine remains still exist in the vicinity.
BAIRDSTOWN, a town of Kentucky, U. S., cap. of Nelson co., 40 m. S of Frankfort, on the E side of Beech fork. In the vicinity there is a Roman Catholic college. Pop. 1,492.

BAIREUTH, or BAYREUTH, a town of Bayaria, cap. of the circle of the Upper Maine, pleasantly situated in a valley on the l. bank of the Red Maine, 25 m. E of Bamberg, at an alt. of 1,174 ft. above sealevel. It is surrounded by old walls, and has three suburbs, comprising the small town of St. George. The river is crossed by two bridges. The streets are broad and regular, and the houses well-built. B. contains a handsome modern castle, two Lutheran churches, a Calvinist and a Roman Catholic church, a synagogue, an hospital, an orphan's asylum, a houseof-correction, a lunatic asylum, a normal school, an academy founded in 1664, a college, a royal library, a gymnasium, and a theatre. The bank, barracks, and riding-school, are handsome edifices; but the principal architectural adornment of the town is the ancient castle of Sophienberg, formerly the residence of the princes of Baireuth. B. possesses an active trade, chiefly in grain, and has manufactories of cotton, linen, and woollen fabrics, hosiery, buttons, porcelain, parchment, tobacco-pipes, sealing-wax, and turnery. It has also excellent breweries, distilleries, tanneries, taweries, tan and fullers'-mills, and printing establishments. A village in the vicinity marks the site of the ancient town of B., and at the distance of half a league are the eastle and beautiful park of the Hermitage, the former built by a margrave of the last century. B. became incorporated with Bavaria in 1810. Pop. in 1831, 2,957 families, and 13,030 individuals, of whom 11,600 were Protestants, 900 Catholics, and 530 Jews.
BAIRRO, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of San Paulo, 3 m. N of San Sebastian.
BAIRRO DAS SILVEIRAS, a town and parish of Brazil, in the prov. of San Paulo, and district of Lorena. Pop. 2,000.
BAIRUT. See BEIRUT.

BAIS, or BAYS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, and cant. of La Guerche. Pop. 3,867.—Also a cant., com., and town of France, in the dep. and arrond. of Mayenne. The cant. comprises 9 com., and contained, in 1831, a pop. of 15,744. The town is 14 m. ESE of Mayenne. Pop. 2,345.

BAISE, or BAYSE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of the Hautes-Pyrenees, in the uplands of the cant. of Lannemezan, and running to the N, crosses the dep. of Gers, passing in its course the towns of Trie Mirande, Valence, and Condom; entering the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, it passes Nerac on the r., and after receiving the Losse, with its affluent the Gelize, unites with the Garonne 2 m. SSW of Aiguillon, and 18 m. WNW of Agen. The entire length of its course is about 150 m., of which 20 from Nerac to the Garonne have, by means of several locks or sluices, been rendered navigable. The principal affluents on the r. are the Baisolle and the Baise Devant. Brandy, farina, cork, &c., form the chief articles of transit on this river. The extension of its navigation was first attempted by Henry IV. In the beginning of the present century the work was renewed; but since 1814 it has been suspended.
BAISIEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, and cant. of Lannoy. Pop. 1,808.
BAISWEIL, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, on one of the head-streams of the Mindel, 8 m. SSE of Mindelheim.
BAISY-THY, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Brabant, and arrond. of Nivelles, which contains the ruins of the castle in which Godfrey of Bouillon was born. Pop. 1,200.
BAIT. See BET.
BAITA, a town of Mexico, in the prov. of Sonora, 65 m. SE of Culiacum.
BAITMAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, 15 m. NW of Mow, and 35 of Ougein. In 1820 it consisted of about 1,500 houses, and was then the cap. of a pergunnah of the same name.

BAITUL, or BEITUL, a large fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundwana, near the source of the Towa, 65 m. SSE of the junction of that river with the Nerbudda, and 90 m. NW of Nagpur, to the rajah of which it belongs.
BAITOS, a town of the island of Hayti, near the southern coast, on a stream which flows into the bay of Occa, 15 m. of Neiva.
BAIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, and cant. of Chomerac, on the r. bank of the Rhone, 9 m. ESE of Privas. Pop. 1,314.
BAIXAS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenees-Orientales, and cant. of Rivesaltes. The environs afford excellent red wines. Pop. 1,840.

BAIXO AMAZONAS, a comarca of Brazil, in the prov. of Para, comprising a vast extent of the territory frequently called Brazilian Guayana. Its principal towns are Barcellos, Macapa, Mamaos, and Obidos. Pop. 30,000.

BAJA, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Bacs- Bodrogh, near the l. bank of the Danube, 30 m. N of Zombor. It contains a fine castle, belonging to the princes of Grassalkovics, a Greek and a Catholic church, a synagogue, and a Franciscan gymnasium. Pop. 11,130.
BAJA. See BAIA.
BAJA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Moldavia, and on the l. bank of the Moldava river, 60 m. WNW of Jassv.

BAJA-DE-RAMA, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the dist. of Little Wallachia, on one of the headstreams of the Motru, 25 m. E of Tirgochil, and 70 m. NW of Krajova. In the vicinity is a copper-mine. BAJADORE (CAPE), BOJADOR, OF BOXEADOR, a BAK cape near the NW extremity of the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 18° 42', and E long. 120° 52'. It forms a low promontory, and is surrounded by an extensive reef of rocks.
BAJANO, or Bosano, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavora, in the dist. and 5 m. E of Nola. Pop. 2,806.
BAJANO. See BOJANO.
BAJERA, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the prov. of Diarbekir, 50 m. SE of Orfa.
BAJETPUR, or BAGITPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the presidency of Bengal, and dist. of Mymunsing, 48 m. NE of Dacca.
BAJEVO-JELO, a town of Slavonia, on the 1. bank of the Save, 42 m. W of Mitrovitz, and about an equal distance ESE of Brod.

BAJIBO (OLD and NEW), two towns on the river Niger, on the opposite banks, between Rabbah and Busab, in 9° 30' N lat. A little below this, the river is 300 yards wide, with soundings from 5 to 6 fath.; but immediately above, the channel is contracted by rocks to 50 yards, and Captain Becroft found it impossible to take his steamer of 30 horse-power much higher. The people of B. brought yams and firewood for sale; but, with the exception of a little ivory, had no produce to barter for merchandise.

BAJMOTZ, or Bojnicz, a town of Hungary, on the r. bank of the Neutra, 25 m. WNW of Neu Sohl. It has a military hospital, and sulphureous thermal springs and baths. In the vicinity is the castle of the counts of Palffy. The manufacture of saddle-trees, and other articles in wood, is extensively carried on in the environs.

BAJUR, a district and town of Afghanistan, divided from Kafiristan by a spur of the Hindukush, and communicating on the S with the Eusufzec country by the valley of the Lundi river. The cap. of the same name is a town of about 5,000 inhabitants, in N lat. 35°.

BAKABANYA, a town of Austria, in Hungary, on the Gran river, 25 m. E of Neutra. Gold and silver have been mined here for upwards of four centuries. The ore, which is of rich quality, is found in a mountain of argillaceous schist, dipping into the great plain which extends towards Tirnaw and the Danube.—Clarke, vol. viii.

BAKAHANY, a village of Russian Persia, in the peninsula of Apcheron, 9 m. N of Baku, near to which exist the principal sources of the black naphtha for which this region is celebrated. In the neighbourhood of the v., the soil is composed of a stratum of clay, partly impregnated with naphtha, under which is successively a stratum of brown freestone containing petrifactions, one of bituminous schist, then one of sand, and under this a stratum of bituminous schist on a mass of white clay, through which run the veins of naphtha. Pits from 3 ft. to 100 ft. in depth are excavated in this soil, and the naphtha collects in these to the extent sometimes of 140 poods in 24 hours. In general these pits are most productive in dry weather. Their total annual produce is about 240,000 poods of black naphtha; and about 900 poods of white naphtha are obtained from pits in the neighbourhood of the v. of Surukhani. The whole of these springs, with the exception of one, belong to government; and were rented in 1820 by a private individual at an annual rent of about £8,200. See BAKU.
BAKARI (CAPE), a point of land on the W coast of Greece, on the N side of the entrance to the gulf of Patras, in N lat. 38° 17′ 15", E long. 21° 30′ 40".
BAKBULAN, a river of Russia, in the Kirghiz steppes, rising in the Khaltai mountains, and flowing into Lake Shin.
BAKARRIKARRI, a tribe of Kosas in S Africa, whose coun try, consisting of extensive plains and forests, lies to the NW of that of the Barolongs .- Campbell.
BAKEL, a town of Holland, in N Brabant, 3 m. NE of Helmont. Pop. 1,549.
BAKER, a county in the state of Georgia, U.S., on the Flint river, in the SW part of the state. The chief productions are cotton and sugar. Pop. 4,226.
BAKER'S DOZEN ISLANDS, a group of islands in Hudson's bay, in about 57° 30' N lat., W long. 81°.
BAKER'S FALLS, a rapid on Hudson's river, in Washington co., New York, in which the river descends 70 ft. in 100 rods.

BAKER'S ISLAND, an islet off Salem harbour, in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 5 m. ENE of Salem.—Also an island off the coast of Maine in N lat. 44° 34'.—Also one of the Georgian islands in the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 74° 55', W long. 97° 30'.
BAKER'S RIVER, a branch of the Pemigewasset river, in Grafton co., New Hampshire, U. S.
BAKERSFIELD, a township in Franklin co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 30 m. NE of Burlington. It is watered by small branches of the Missisque. Pop. 1,258.

BAKEWELL, a parish and market-town of Derbyshire, in the hund. of High Peak.—The p. includes the chapelries of Ashford, Baslow, Beeley, Buxton, Chelmorton, Great Longstone with Holme, Mony-Ash, Sheldon, and Taddington with Priestcliffe, and the townships of Bakewell, Blackwell, Brushfield, Bubnell, Flagg, Froggart, Harthill, Over and Nether Haddon, Rowland, Great Rowsley with part of Alport, and part of Wardlow, and the hamlets of Calver, Curbar, Hassop, and Little Longstone. It is upwards of 20 m. in length, and 8 m. in breadth; and has an area of 43,020 acres. Mines of coal, lead, and zinc, and quarries of stone and marble, give employment to many of the inhabitants. In 1838 there were two cotton-mills in this parish, employing 393 hands. Pop. in 1801, 9,162; in 1831, 9,503; in 1841, 10,363. In this parish is Chatsworth-house, the celebrated seat of the Duke of Devonshire, and one of the finest modern mansions in England, distant about 3 m. from the market-town of Bakewell; and to the S of Bakewell, at the distance of 2 m., on the E side of the Wye, stands Haddon-hall, a magnificent baronial mansion of the 14th and 15th cent., still entire though no longer inhabited. The market-town of BAKEWELL, in the above parish, is situated on the river Wye, near its confluence with the Derwent; 153 m. NW of London, 12 m. SW of Chesterfield, and 22 m. NW of Derby, in a beautiful and picturesque vale between Matlock and Buxton. It stands on a rising ground, on the r. bank of the Wye, about 2½ m. above its confluence with the Derwent. It is the place of nomination, and a polling-place, for a member for North Derby. The town is under the jurisdiction of the county magistrates. The smelting of lead is carried on to a considerable extent here. The original name of this town is derived from the Saxon Badecanwillan, -of which it is a contraction and corruption, -a name given to it from the circumstance of there being a mineral well here, the waters of which are supposed to have been in repute prior to the year 924. The water is chalybeate; and a fine set of baths have been established here by the Duke of Rutland. The temp. of the water is 60° Fahr. Pop., in 1801, 1,412; in 1831, 1,898; in 1841, 1,976. Houses in 1841, 403. Area of township, 3,380 acres.
BAKEWELL (Mount), a conical hill in W Australia, in the co. of York, on the route from Perth to Mount Stirling. The town of York is laid out round the base of this hill.

BAKHTCHISSARAI, or BAKTSHISERAI, [i. e. BAK 'the Palace of the Gardens,'] a town in the Russian gov. of Taurida, in N lat. 44° 59', the ancient Tartar cap. of the Crimea. It is prettily situated in a narrow deep valley,-"a prodigious natural fosse," [Clarke], - running SSE and NNW, enclosed on both sides with high rocky terraces of cretaceous limestone, and intersected by the Churuksa, an affluent of the Katsha. The houses rise in terraces along the sides of the hills, interspersed with gardens, vineyards, and clumps of black poplar trees; but the streets are narrow, crooked, and dirty. In 1822, the pop. amounted to 10,212, of whom 8,200 were Mahommedans. No Russian is permitted to settle in the town; but there are a few Armenian families. The manufactures of B. consist of leather, saddlery, pottery, cutlery, silks, and bouza, a spirit distilled from millet. The khan's palace is the principal building in the town. It is carefully kept up in the oriental form.—Pallas.—Clarke.—Lyall.

BAKHTEGAN, a salt lake of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, lying to the E of Shiraz, near the town of Niriz. The Kur or Bundemir falls into it, and all those streams which water the village of Morgab, Merdasht, and Karbal. It is nearly 60 m. in length from WNW to ESE, but is of little breadth, and is nearly dry in summer. It is surrounded on all sides by a saline desert. It is not mentioned by ancient writers.

BAKHTIYARI MOUNTAINS, a range of mountains in Persia, separating the prov. of Khuzistan and Luristan. They are inhabited by a wild nomadic race, who speak a rude dialect of the Persian; and support themselves chiefly by their flocks of sheep and cattle. They bear a bad reputation; and are such notorious thieves and robbers that the name of Bakhtiyar is become synonymous with these terms. [De Bode.] Morier gives them a somewhat better character. He says they are a brave hardy race of people, and that their language has much of the old Farsi in it. They calculate their numbers at about 100,000 houses; and say that they originally came from Rum, i. e. Turkey. They are Mahommedans of the Shiah sect.—De Bode's Travels.—Geog. Journ. vols. vii. and ix.

BAKIR, or Bakyr, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Karasi, on the Bakir-shai, the ancient Caicus. BAKIR KUREH-SI, [i. e. 'the Copper district,'] a district and town of Asiatic Turkey. The town, of about 200 houses, is in N lat. 41° 47', E long. 33° 50', in a deep hollow surrounded by mountains, but at an alt. of 2,800 ft. above sea-level. The district was at one time celebrated for its copper mines; but they are no longer wrought. A few thieves and convicts only are engaged in sifting and collecting from the vast piles of the refuse of former workings such pieces as may be again sent to the furnace with some chance of profit. The peak of Bakir-Sultan rises 710 ft. above the town.—Ainsworth.

BAKIR-SHAI, a river of Asiatic Turkey, which rises on the W side of the Kodja-dagh, in several streams, and flows W by S, through the plain of Pergamus, receiving the Berghama-shai a little to the S of the modern Berghama, to the Elaitic gulf into which it flows a little to the W of the ancient Elea. This stream seems to be the ancient Caicus.
BAKKIL, a town of Nubia, in Dongola, on the l. bank of the Nile, in N lat. 18° 10'.

BAKKUR, or B'HAKAR, a town of Sind, on a rocky island in the Indus river, opposite to the town of Rohri, in N lat. 27° 42', E long. 69° 2', which Rennell identifies with the Mansurah of Arabian geographers. There is a fort here of imposing appearance. There are numerous Mahommedan tombs and shrines in the neighbourhood.—Masson.
BAKMUD. See BACHMUTH.
BAKNANG. See BACKNANG.

BAKONYER-WALD, or BAKONY MOUNTAINS, a range of forest-clad mountains in Hungary, running from SW to NE in the comitat of Wesprim, on the N of Lake Balaton. They are a branch of the Styrian Alps; and are chiefly inhabited by German colonists who manufacture great quantities of articles of turnery, wooden implements, and that species of tinder which is yielded by a species of Boletus. The prevailing tree is the oak; few pines occur in it.
BAKSHIDWAR, or Buxidwar, a pass and village in the country of Bhutan, in N lat. 26° 52', E long. 89° 38'.

BAKU, or Bachu, a port of Russia, on the Caspian, at the S extremity of the peninsula of Apcheron, the sacred city of the Guebres or Fireworshippers. The town is built upon an acclivity, and is walled. Its pop. is between 4,000 and 5,000; and it has a considerable commerce in naphtha, saffron, madder, silk, and salt, all of which are extensively produced in the peninsula,—an elevated rocky tract, abounding in volcanic phenomena. "The neighbourhood of B., as well as the islands in the bay of B.," says Mr. Eichwald, "every where display rocks of shell-limestone of the tertiary period. The soil through which the 'perpetual fire' of B. rushes, is a shell-limestone, in which we observe many shells, but can distinguish only small cardia. Its colour is often blackish, which may arise from the penetration of the naphtha, for it has a strong naphthic smell. It is disposed in horizontal strata, sometimes porous, and appears as if fire had acted on it. Other limestones occur, with conchoidal fracture, and with few or no shells; but, as we approach the naphtha springs, the limestone becomes less and less abundant; at length its place is taken by a blackish earthy loam which is deeply impregnated with naphtha. The naphtha pits or wells are here very numerous, and differ in depth. The best colourless thin naphtha shows by the aræometer 183°; the worst, and consequently the thickest, only 11 per cent.: the one is pure and perfectly liquid; the other, when poured from one vessel into another, is very tenacious. Here, also, occurs the great salt lake, the Massassir, about 15 versts from B., which, when at its greatest height, is about 5 versts long, and half as broad. Its circumference is about 12 versts. Every summer it affords about 150,000 pood of salt, but, in case of urgency, could yield 320,000 pood. The lake of Sich, about 17 versts from B., is only 13 verst long, and about 1 verst broad, and 5 versts in circumf. It never dries up. The salt forms a layer on the bottom three inches thick, and annually affords 20,000 pood, but 200,000 could be collected from it. The other lakes, many in number, afford annually 160,000 pood, but could produce 566,000, if there was demand for the salt. The perpetual fire, about 15 versts NE from Baku, in front of the town of Suruk-hani, is not, as earlier travellers maintain, burning naphtha, but hydrogen gas (probably carbonated hydrogen), which rises through cracks and openings of the calcareous rocks, and on the approach of a flame, takes fire, and continues to burn. It never takes fire spontaneously, nor by the approach of red coal, if not burning with flame. The gas, as it escapes from the rock, is without smell-is not sensibly warm-and, on being respired, does not occasion any disagreeable feeling. It burns with a yellowish-white flame, and forms with atmospheric air an exploding gas. This perpetual fire, worshipped by the holy Indians of Baku, does not differ from other ephemeral phenomena of the same kind known in other parts of the world. Naphtha volcanoes, in BAL in several of the islands on the W coast of the Caspian. They agree pretty nearly with the mud-volcanoes in the peninsula of Kertsch, and the isle of Taman, described by Pallas; and deserve more the name of naphtha-volcanoes than mud-volcanoes, as their eruption always terminates with a pouring out of naphtha. Near to B. about one-fourth verst from the perpetual fire, a heat rises out of a fissure of the shell-limestone, which is so strong that the hand can scarcely bear it: hence, from all these circumstances, we can scarcely doubt of the existence of a subterranean heating process in the peninsula of Apcheron."

BALA, a town in the parish of Llan-y-kil, co. of Merioneth, near the N end of the lake of the same name, 37 m. NW by W of Shrewsbury. It consists for the most part of one wide street, with a few handsome houses. It is one of the polling-places for the election of the member for Merionethshire; and the assizes for the co. are held here and at Dolgelly alternately. The principal manufacture carried on in the town is that of knitted gloves, stockings, and worsted wigs. Pop. in 1821, 1,162.
BALA, a town of Austria, in Hungary, 25 m. E of Funfkirchen, on the Danube.-Also a town of Senegambia, in the Futatora territory, in N lat. 15° 14'.
BALABAC, an island about 15 m. in length, by 4 m. in breadth, lying off the SW extremity of the island of Pulawan, in N lat. 8°, E long. 117°.
BALABAGH, a town of Afghanistan, 15 m. W of Jellalabad, in N lat. 34° 22'. It is celebrated for its fruits, and fine sweetmeats.

BALABALAGAN, a cluster of small low islets in the straits of Macassar, between Borneo and Celebes, sometimes named the LITTLE PATERNOSTER ISLES. The NW island of the group is in S lat. 2° 8', E long. 117° 41'.
BALABEA ISLANDS, a group off the coast of New Caledonia, in the S Pacific, in S lat. 20°, E long. 164° 20'.

BALACHNA, or BALAKNA, a district of Russia, in the prov. of Nishgorod, on the r. bank of the Volga. It is fertile and well-cultivated. Its cap., of the same name, is in N lat. 56° 15', E long. 43° 30', 25 m. NW of Nishni-Novgorod. It has a pop. of about 4,000.
BALAG, an islet off the NE point of Samar, in the Philippine group, in N lat. 12° 40', E long. 125° 10'.
BALAGANSK, a town of Siberia, 60 m. NW of Irkutsk, on the l. bank of the Angora. Pop. in 1833, 372.

BALAGHAUTS, or BALAG'HA'T, [1. e. 'Country above,' or 'High lands,'] a district of Hindostan, in the central plateau of the Mysore country, between N lat. 13° 15' and 16° 20', and E long. 75° 40' and 79° 20', so called from its being situated above the Ghauts, and extending from the river Krishna to the river Kaveri, or the S extremity of Mysore. The term is usually restricted to what are called 'the Ceded districts,' acquired by treaty with the Nizam in 1800, forming only a small portion of this great table-land. These districts, comprising an area of about 25,500 sq. m., are bounded on the N by the rivers Krishna and Tumbudra, which separate them from the Northern Circars, and are the only large rivers of the country; the southern valleys lie amongst the Ghauts. The soil is fertile; but the central districts are subject to oppressive droughts, large tracts are rugged, and there is a great deficiency of wood. The productions are indigo, sugar, cotton, tobacco, betel, and red pepper. Cattle, sheep, and goats, are reared in great numbers. The central and eastern districts contain rich diamond mines. The pop. in 1806 amounted to 1,917,376, and has a state of activity, occur at B. and at Sallian, as also | greatly increased since under British administration. Only about a fifteenth part of the pop. are Mahommedans. In 1836-7 the revenue amounted to £536,273. The Ceded districts are now, with the addition of Pungepur, and Gudiput, divided into the collectorates of Bellary and Cuddapah. The Hindu subdivision included Adoni, Kurnul, Cummin, Bellary, Harponelly, Rydrug, Guti, Ghazopur, Cuddapah, Gurtumcondah, Sidhut, and Dupaud.—B. formed the ancient Hindu empire of Karnata, and was included in the last Hindu empire of Bijnagur. After the fall of the empire of Delhi it was divided among several independent chiefs. Hyder added it to his dominions; and after his fall, the Nizam got possession of it in his wars with the rajah of Mysore in 1792 and 1799. See Bellary and Cuddapah.
BALAGIECH, a mountain in the SE of the co. of Renfrew, in the p. of Eaglesham, rising 1,000 ft. above sea-level, which, besides sulphate of barytes, is said to contain lead and silver ore.
BALAGONAN (POINT), a headland on the W coast of the island of Mindanao, in N lat. 7° 45', E long. 122° 15'.

BALAGUER, a small town of Spain, in Catalonia, on the r. bank of the Segre, at the foot of a steep hill, in a tract of uncommon fertility, 16 m. NE of Lerida. It contains several convents, a castle, and 4,642 inhabitants. In 1411, when Ferdinand of Castile was elected king of Aragon, the count of Urgel, one of his rival competitors, threw himself into this town, but was compelled to surrender at discretion; he suffered, however, perpetual imprisonment, and his estate was confiscated. In 1709, B. was taken by Stahremberg; and in 1710, by the duke de Vendome. The partido of B. comprises 123 pueblos.
BALAGUER (COL DE), a pass on the SE coast of Catalonia, commanding the high road from Tarragona to the mouth of the Ebro.
BALAGUERES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Arriege, cant. of Castillon. Pop. 1,289.
BALAHORE, a small town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Erzerum, between Baibut and Gunushkana.
BALAIGNE, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, to the W of Orbe. Pop. 350.

BALAKLA'VA, a small seaport of Russia, at the SW point of the Crimea, in N lat. 44° 50', E long. 33° 36', — the Symbolorum Portus of Strabo. The harbour is well sheltered, has great depth of water, and is capable of containing 12 sail-of-theline; but difficult of access,—being still the "portus angusto introitu" of Strabo; its entrance not exceeding 30 yards in width. From the town it appears like a small Scottish loch, land-locked by high and precipitous mountains. It abounds with mackerel and mullet which are extensively fished here. Upon the heights above the mouth of the port are the ruins of a magnificent Genoese fortress; and the mountain upon the NE side has been excavated into "stately magazines and chambers" whose sides are lined with coloured stucco in the highest preservation. [Clarke.] The mountains surrounding the port are of red and white marble; and the shore yields a fine glittering sand of gold-coloured mica. Clarke considers the vale of B. as hardly surpassed by any scene in the Crimea. The name appears to be a corruption of the Italian translation,—Bella chiave, —of the Greek appellation Καλός Λιμήν, i. e. 'the Beautiful port.' The town, now containing about 1,750 inhabitants, was colonized by Greek and Albanian refugees, under the auspices of Catherine II.; and with its Greek pop., its girdle of rocks, and its mild climate, much resembles those little towns of the archipelago that are seen specking the horizon as one sails towards Constantinople. [H. de Hell.] BAL
BALALUAN, a volcano in the island of Sumatra, situated in the northern part of the island, near Acheen.
BALAM (Pulo), a mountainous island off the W coast of the Malay peninsula, in N lat. 6° 35', E long. 99°. It is separated by a very narrow strait on the W from Pulo Buton.
BALAMATTA, a town on the E coast of the island of Bouro, one of the Moluccas, in S lat. 3° 8'.

BALAMBANGAN, a rich and fruitful island 15 m. distant from the N extremity of Borneo, and to the W of Banguey island. Its N point is in N lat. 7° 21½, and E long. 117° 3½. It is about 14 m. in length from SE to NW, and from 3 m. to 6 m. in breadth; and has two harbours. Being ceded by the king of Sulu to the East India company, a settlement was established upon it in 1773. But the Sulu people surprised it in 1774, and destroyed the settlement. A new establishment was formed in 1803, which proving expensive, without promising to be of any real advantage, was withdrawn.

BALAMBUAN, or PALAMBUAN, a district in the SE part of the island of Java, along the shore of the straits of Bali. The settlement of B. stands on a river of the same name; and consists of a fort, protected by a ditch, and surrounded by a village, in S lat. 8° 28'.
BALAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Sedan. Pop. 1,068.
BALANCE, an islet in the English channel, off the coast of Brest, to the NW of the isle of Molem, in N lat. 48° 24', W long. 4° 52'.
BALANGA, a town in the island of Luzon, in the Philippine group, on the S coast, opposite the Mindoro shores, in N lat. 13° 40', E long. 121° 15'.

BALANGIGNI, an island in the Sulu archipelago, for many years the resort of a daring horde of pirates, whom the Manilla authorities severely chastised in the month of February 1848. The Spaniards on this occasion liberated about 200 slaves, Dutch and Spanish subjects, took 124 guns, and destroyed 150 piratical prows, besides razing 7 villages, and 4 fortresses.
BALANGODE, a town in the island of Ceylon, 50 m. SE of Candy, in N lat. 6° 20'. It is nearly 2,000 ft. above sea-level.
BALANIPA, a town in the island of Celebes, upon the W coast, in S lat. 3° 15', E long. 119° 20'.

BALANTES, an island off the coast of W Africa. It is separated from the main by a narrow channel, and is one of a numerous group. The inhabitants are described as a fierce and savage race, disinclined to hold communication with their neighbours.-Also a river of Senegambia, the boundary between the Biafars and Mandingoes, which falls into the straits that separate the Balantes islands, in N lat. 12°, W long. 16° 20'. Its course is imperfectly known, but it appears to inosculate with the Rio Grande.

BALA POOL, also called LLYN TEGID, or PIMBLE-MERE, a lake in the co. of Merioneth, 4 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth, with an average depth of 40 ft.; forming the source of the noble river Dee, which issues from its NE extremity. It is the largest lake in North Wales, and is well-supplied with pike, eels, tench, trout, and the fish called the gwyniad, which resembles salmon. The scenery in the immediate neighbourhood is uninteresting; but the view in the distance is grand.
BALARDO (RIO DE SAN), a river of NW America, falling into the North Pacific in N lat. 34° 40', W long. 120°.
BALARUC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Herault, cant. of Frontignan, near the banks of Lake Thau; 15 m. SW of Montpellier. Here are warm baths of a temp. of 42° Reaumur.
BALARUD. See ABI-BALAD-RUD.
BALA-SALLA, a village in the p. of Mallew, isle of Man, 2 m. from Castletown. Pop. 516. The ruins of Mac-Manis' Abbey, founded in 1098, are in the environs.

BALASCHEV, or BALASCHEW, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Saratov, upon the Khoper, a tributary to the Don. Pop. 2,000. To the N of this town are extensive forests; to the S, interminable heaths or steppes.
BALASFALVA, or Blasendorf, a town of Austria, in Transylvania, near the conflux of the Great and Little Kokel rivers; 26 m. NW of Hermanstadt. It is the residence of the bishop of Fogaras.
BALASINORE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujerat, 41 m. E of Ahmedabad.

BALASORE, a seaport town of Hindostan, in the Cuttack district of the prov. of Orissa. It is situated on the banks of the Buri Balang, 125 m. SW of Calcutta, in N lat. 21° 32', E long. 86° 56'. The English, Dutch, and Portuguese, had factories here for the manufacture of fine cotton cloths at an early period. There are graving-docks here capable of admitting vessels whose draught does not exceed 14 ft. The chief trade of the place is in corn and salt. It is in Balasore roads that the Calcutta pilots wait the arrival of vessels; it is a dangerous navigation, and requires great skill to take ships up the Ganges from this point. The road-distance to Calcutta is 141 m.

BALASSA-GYARMATH, a town of Hungary, in the circle of Neograd, on the Ipoly. Pop. 4,265, many of whom are Jews. BALAT, BALATIA, or PALATIA, a village of Asiatic Turkey, in the sanjak of Siglah, on the r. bank of the Mæander, 14 m. S of Priene, supposed by some to mark the site of the ancient Miletus.
BALATELLO POINT, a cape on the NW coast of the island of Buro, in the Molucca sea, in S lat. 2° 49′.
BALATOF, a town of Asiatic Russia, on the river Khoper, 146 m. from Saratov.

BALATON, or Platten See, a lake in the SW of Hungary, lying between the counties of Szalad, Wesprim, and Schumeg, 5 m. S of Stuhl-Weissenburg. It is first formed by the small river Szala which flows into it at its S extremity; and is augmented by a number of rivulets, until it becomes nearly 40 m. in length from SW to NE, and from 1 m. to 4 m. broad. Its general depth is from 26 ft. to 36 ft., and it occupies a surface of 120 sq. m., to which may be added nearly as much more of swamps and marshes. It discharges its waters by the river Sio, a sleepy unnavigable stream, or rather a mere swamp which, afterwards, by the accession of different streams, has its torpidity roused into action, and becomes a stream flowing into the Danube. One of the sides of the B. is high and mountainous,—the other level and swampy; on the ridges of the former, stretching from E to W and facing the S, the vine is successfully cultivated. The water of this lake may, it is alleged, be preserved for nearly two years without putrifying. It contains, besides the fishes common in the country, several of rare occurrence; it is frequented also by bitterns, and some singular species of water-fowl. It has long been an object with the Austrian government to unite this lake with the Danube by a canal.
BALAVADON, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in a great plain of the same name, between the Sultantagh on the S, and the Emir-tagh on the N, at an alt. of 2,900 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,000.

BALBASTRO, or BARBASTRO, a town of Spain, in Aragon, near the conflux of the Vero and Cinca, the see of a bishop, suffragan of Saragossa, whose 6' 15", E long. 118° 6' 45". BAL jurisdiction extends over 170 parishes. The inhabitants, who amount to about 6,450, are chiefly employed in tanning leather. It is 30 m. ENE of Saragossa, and 47 m. NW of Barcelona.
BALBEC. See BAALBEC.
BALBEIS, a town of Lower Egypt, in the Delta, 10 m. SW of Mansore.
BALBIGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Nerondo. Pop. 1,230.

BALBRIGGAN, a chapelry, market-town, and sea-port, in the p. of Balrothery, 34 m. NW of Skerries, and 153 m. N by E of Dublin, co. Dublin. It rose from the condition of an obscure fishinghamlet, to that of a seat of manufacture, a considerable seaport, and an incipient town, about the year 1780. About 1,000 persons in B. and its neighbourhood, including Skerries, are partly or chiefly employed in the embroidering of muslin; and upwards of 400 looms are employed in hand-weaving. The pier at the port is nearly 600 ft. in length. The harbour is clean, with a bottom of soft sand, so that vessels may run boldly for it, when there is sufficient water, which, within the pier-head, is about 14 ft. at high-water springs; it is all dry at low water. Many vessels have been saved here in E or SE gales. The town is a summer-resort for sea-bathing. Area of the town, 180 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,016; in 1841, 2,959. Houses 567.
BALBRONN, or Balbrun, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of the Lower Rhine, 15 m. W of Strasburg. Pop. 1,000. Excellent wine is produced in the vicinity.

BALBY, a township in the p. and 2 m. SW of Doncaster, W. R. of Yorkshire. Area with Hexthorp 1,420 acres. Pop. 486. The first meeting of the society of Friends was held here by George Fox and his followers.
BALCAN. See BALKAN.
BALCASH. See BALKASH.
BALCHIK, a port on the gulf of Baba, in the Black sea, between Varna and Cape Kalagria, in N lat. 43° 15'.

BALCOMBE, a parish in the co. of Sussex, 4 m. N of Cuckfield, intersected by the London and Brighton railway which is here carried through a tunnel 1,139 yards in length. Area of p. 6,050 acres. Pop. 1,542.
BALCONDAH, a town of Hindostan, in Hyderabad, 50 m. N of the capital, in N lat. 18° 41'.
BALD, a town of Austria, in Transylvania, upon a tributary to the Marosch; 52 m. NE of Carlsburg.
BALD EAGLE, a river of Pennsylvania, U.S. It originates in Centre co., and, after a course of 50 m., falls into the W branch of the Susquehanna, about 12 m. from Jersey shore.
BALD EAGLE, a township in Clinton co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S. Pop. 1,178.

BALD EAGLE MOUNTAINS, a range in Pennsylvania, U.S. On the E side of this great range lies the valley of Bald Eagle, or of the Sinking spring. It is a picturesque and fertile vale, 5 m. wide, and rendered interesting by a natural phenomenon called the Swallow, which is a rocky cavern into which several large streams descend; after flowing through a subterranean passage of several miles, they appear to discharge their united waters another great aperture.

BALD HEAD, a promontory at the extremity of Norton sound, on the NW coast of America, in N lat. 65°, W long. 162°.—Also a headland of North Carolina, U. S., at the SW extremity of Smith's island, and at the mouth of Cape Fear river, 24 m. NNW of Fryingpan shoal; in N lat. 33° 51', W long. 78° 13'.—Also a promontory on the coast of New Holland, in King George's sound, in S lat. 36°
BALD ISLAND, an island on the S coast of Australia, in S lat. 34° 55', E long. 118° 29'. It is about 5 m. in length, by 2 m. in breadth.

BALDECK, or BALDEGG, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Lucerne, on a lake of the same name, at an alt. of 1,446 ft. above sea-level.—The lake— which is known as the Baldegg-see, Baldegger- SEE, HEIDEGGER-SEE, or REICHEN-SEE—is a small sheet of water, about 2 m. in length, communicating with the Halwyler-see in Aargau.
BALDENBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of West Prussia, circle of Marienwerder, 15 m. NE of New Stettin, on the Ball or Zahne, an affluent of the Kuddow. Pop. in 1837, 1,223.
BALDENHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas Rhin, cant. of Marckolsheim. Pop. 1,004.
BALDERIA, a town of Venetian-Lombardy, in the deleg. of Verona, on the Gua. Pop. 2,500.
